author: John Chrysostom
book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

ccel.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

title: Homily XXIV on Acts x. 44, 46.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “While Peter yet spake these words, the Holy Ghost fell on all them which heard the word.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they of the circumcision which believed were astonished, as many as came with Peter, because that on the Gentiles also was poured out the gift of the Holy Ghost.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they heard them speak with tongues, and magnify God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe God’s providential management.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not suffer the speech to be finished, nor the baptism to take place upon a command of Peter, but, when He has made it evident how admirable their state of mind is, and a beginning is made of the work of teaching, and they have believed that assuredly baptism is the remission of sins, then forthwith comes the Spirit upon them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now this is done by God’s so disposing it as to provide for Peter a mighty ground of justification.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it is not simply that the Spirit came upon them, but, “they spake with tongues:” which was the thing that astonished those who had come together.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They altogether disliked the matter, wherefore it is that the whole is of God; and as for Peter, it may almost be said, that he is present only to be taught (with them) the lesson, that they must take the Gentiles in hand, and that they themselves are the persons by whom this must be done.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For whereas after all these great events, still both in Cæsarea and in Jerusalem a questioning is made about it, how would it have been if these (tokens) had not gone step by step with the progress of the affair?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore it is that this is carried to a sort of excess.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Peter seizes his advantage, and see the plea he makes of it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Can any man forbid water, that these should not be baptized, which have received the Holy Ghost as well as we?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark the issue to which he brings it; how he has been travailing to bring this forth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So (entirely) was he of this mind!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Can any one, he asks, “forbid water?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is the language, we may almost say, of one triumphantly pressing his advantage (ἐπεμβαίνοντος) against such as would forbid, such as should say that this ought not to be.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The whole thing, he says, is complete, the most essential part of the business, the baptism with which we were baptized.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he commanded them to be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: After he has cleared himself, then, and not before, he commands them to be baptized: teaching them by the facts themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such was the dislike the Jews had to it!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore it is that he first clears himself, although the very facts cry aloud, and then gives the command.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then prayed they him”—well might they do so—“to tarry certain days:” and with a good courage thenceforth he does tarry.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the Apostles and brethren that were in Judea heard that the Gentiles had also received the word of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when Peter was come up to Jerusalem, they that were of the circumcision contended with him, saying, Thou wentest in to men uncircumcised, and didst eat with them.” (ch. xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: After such great things, “they of the circumcision contended:” not the Apostles; God forbid!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It means, they took no small offence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see what they allege.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They do not say, Why didst thou preach? but, Why didst thou eat with them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Peter, not stopping to notice this frigid objection—for frigid indeed it is—takes his stand (ἵσταται) on that great argument, If they had the Spirit Itself given them, how could one refuse to give them the baptism?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But how came it that in the case of the Samaritans this did not happen, but, on the contrary, neither before their baptism nor after it was there any controversy, and there they did not take it amiss, nay, as soon as they heard of it, sent the Apostles for this very purpose? (ch. viii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, but neither in the present case is this the thing they complain of; for they knew that it was of Divine Grace: what they say is, Why didst thou eat with them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Besides, the difference is not so great for Samaritans as it is for Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover, it is so managed (as part of the Divine plan) that he is accused in this way: on purpose that they may learn: for Peter, without some cause given, would not have related the vision.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe his freedom from all elation and vainglory.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it says, “But Peter rehearsed the matter from the beginning, and expounded it by order unto them, saying, I was in the city of Joppa, praying:” he does not say why, nor on what occasion: “and in a trance I saw a vision, a certain vessel descend, as it had been a great sheet, let down from heaven by four corners; and it came even to me (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4, 5): upon the which when I had fastened mine eyes, I considered, and saw fourfooted beasts of the earth, and wild beasts, and creeping things, and fowls of the air.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And I heard a voice saying unto me, Arise, Peter; slay and eat.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As much as to say, This of itself was enough to have persuaded me—my having seen the linen sheet: but moreover a Voice was added.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But I said, Not so, Lord: for nothing common or unclean hath at any time entered into my mouth.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I did my part,” says he: “I said, that I have never eaten aught common or unclean:” with reference to this that they said, “Thou wentest in, and didst eat with them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this he does not say to Cornelius: for there was no need to mention it to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But the voice answered me again from heaven, What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this was done three times: and all were drawn up again into heaven.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The essential points were those (that ensued at Cæsarea); but by these he prepares the way for them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he justifies himself (by reasons), and forbears to use his authority as teacher.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the more mildly he expresses himself, the more tractable he makes them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “At no time,” says he, “has aught common or unclean entered into my mouth.—And, behold—this too was part of his defence—three men stood at the house in which I was, sent to me from Cæsarea.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the Spirit bade me go with them, nothing doubting.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark that it is to the Spirit the enacting of laws belongs!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And these also accompanied me”—nothing can be more lowly, when he alleges the brethren for witnesses!—“these six men, and we entered into the man’s house: and he showed us how he had seen an angel in his house, which stood and said unto him, Send men to Joppa, and call for Simon, whose surname is Peter; who shall tell thee words, whereby thou and all thy house shall be saved.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not mention the words spoken by the Angel to Cornelius, “Thy prayers and thine alms are come up for a memorial before God, that he may not disgust them; but what says he?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He shall tell thee words, whereby thou and all thy house shall be saved:” with good reason this is added.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Also he says nothing of the man’s fitness (ἐπιεικές).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The Spirit,” he might say, “having sent (me), God having commanded, on the one part having summoned (me) through the Angel, on the other urging (me) on, and solving my doubt about the things, what was I to do?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He says none of these things, however: but makes his strong point of what happened last, which even in itself was an incontrovertible argument.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And as I began to speak,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then why did not this happen alone?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of superabundance (ἐκ περιουσίας) this is wrought by God, that it might be shown that the beginning too was not from the Apostle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But had he set out of his own motion, without any of these things having taken place, they would have been very much hurt: so that from the beginning he disposes their minds in his favor**: saying to them, “Who have received the Holy Ghost even as we.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And not content with this, he reminds them also of the words of the Lord: “Then remembered I the word of the Lord, how that he said, John indeed baptized with water; but ye shall be baptized with the Holy Ghost.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He means, that no new thing has happened, but just what the Lord foretold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But there was no need to baptize?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the baptism was completed already.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, I ordered them to be baptized: but what says he?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Forasmuch then as God gave them the like gift as He did unto us, who believed on the Lord Jesus Christ; what was I, that I could withstand God?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows that he had himself done nothing: for the very thing which we have obtained, he says, that same did those men receive.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That he may more effectually stop their mouths, therefore he says, “The like gift.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you perceive how he does not allow them to have less: when they believed, says he, the same gift did God give unto them, as He did to us who believed on the Lord, and Himself cleanses them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, To you, but to us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why do you feel aggrieved, when we call them partakers (with us?)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “When they heard these things, they held their peace, and glorified God, saying, Then hath God also to the Gentiles granted repentance unto life.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark that it all came of Peter’s discourse, by his admirably skilful way of relating the facts?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They glorified God that He had given repentance to themselves (καὶ αὐτοῖς) also: they were humbled by these words.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hence was the door of faith opened thenceforth to the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, if you please, let us look over again what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “While Peter yet spake,” etc. (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not say that Peter was astonished, but, “They of the circumcision:” since he knew what was in preparation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet they ought to have marvelled at this, how they themselves had believed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When they heard that they had believed, they were not astonished, but when God gave them the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then “answered Peter and said,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And therefore it is that he says, “God hath shown that I should not call common or unclean any human being.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He knew this from the first, and plans his discourse beforehand (with a view to it).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What Gentiles henceforth?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They were no longer Gentiles, the Truth being come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is nothing wonderful, he says, if before the act of baptism they received the Spirit: in our own case this same happened.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Peter shows that not as the rest either were they baptized, but in a much better way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the reason why the thing takes place in this manner, that they may have nothing to say, but even in this way may account them equal with themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they besought him,” it says, “to tarry certain days.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the Apostles and brethren, etc. And they of the circumcision contended with him.” (ch. xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you remark how they were not kindly disposed towards him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Saying “Thou wentest in to men uncircumcised, and didst eat with them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you note what zeal they had for the Law?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not Peter’s authority abashed them, not the signs which had taken place, not the success achieved, what a thing it was, the Gentiles having “received the word:” but they contended about those petty things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if none of those (signs) had taken place, was not the success (itself) enough?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But not so does Peter frame his defence: for he was wise, or rather it was not his wisdom, but the Spirit that spake the words.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And by the matter of his defence, he shows that in no one point was he the author, but in every point God, and upon Him he casts the whole.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The trance,” he says—“it was He that caused me to fall into it, for “I was in Joppa,” etc.: the vessel—it was He that showed it; I objected: again, He spake, and even then I did not hear: the Spirit commanded me to go, and even then though I went, I did not run: I told that God had sent me, and after these things, even then I did not baptize, but again God did the whole.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God baptized them, not I.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, Was it not right then to add the water? but, implying that nothing was lacking, “What was I, that I should withstand God?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What a defence is here!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he does not say, Then knowing these things, hold your peace; but what?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He stands their attack, and to their impeachment he pleads—“What was I, to be able to hinder God?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was not possible for me to hinder—a forcible plea indeed, and such as might well put them to shame.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence being at last afraid, “they held their peace and glorified God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In like manner ought we also to glorify God for the good things which befall our neighbors, only not in the way that the rest of the newly-baptized are insulted, when they see others receiving baptism, and immediately departing this life.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It, is right to glorify God, even though all be saved: and as for thee, if thou be willing, thou hast received a greater gift (than they): I do not mean in respect of the baptism, for the gift there is the same for him as for thee, but in regard that thou hast received a set time for winning distinction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The other put on the robe, and was not suffered to exhibit himself therewith in the procession, whereas to thee, God hath given full opportunity to use thine arms for the right purpose, thereby to make proof of them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The other goes his way, having only the reward of his faith: thou standest in the course, both able to obtain an abundant recompense for thy works, and to show thyself as much more glorious than he, as the sun is than the smallest star, as the general, nay rather as the Emperor himself, than the lowest soldier.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then blame thyself, or rather not blame, but correct: for it is not enough to blame thyself; it is in thy power to contend afresh.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hast thou been thrown? hast thou taken grievous hurt?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Stand up, recover thyself: thou art still in the course, the meeting (θέατρον) is not yet broken up.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you not see how many that have been thrown in the wrestling have afterwards resumed the combat?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Only do not willingly come by thy fall.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Dost thou count him a happy man for departing this life?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Much rather count thyself happy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Was he released of his sins?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But thou, if thou wilt, shalt not only wash away thy sins, but shalt also have achievements (of good works), which in his case is not possible.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is in our power to recover ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great are the medicinal virtues (φάρμακα) of repentance: let none despair of himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That man truly deserves to be despaired of, who despairs of himself; that man has no more salvation, nor any hopes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not the having fallen into a depth of evils, it is the lying there when fallen, that is dreadful, it is not the having come into such a condition, it is the making light of it that is impious.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The very thing that ought to make thee earnest, say, is it this that makes thee reckless?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having received so many wounds, hast thou fallen back?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of the soul, there can be no incurable wound; for the body, there are many such, but none for the soul: and yet for those we cease not in our endeavors to cure them, while for these we are supine.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou not the thief (on the cross), in how short a time he achieved (his salvation)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou not the Martyrs, in how short a time they accomplished the whole work?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But martyrdom is not to be had nowadays.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, but there are contests to be had, as I have often told you, if we had the mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For they that wish,” says the Apostle, “to live godly in Christ Jesus, shall suffer persecution.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (2 Tim. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They that live godly are always undergoing persecution, if not from men, at any rate from evil spirits, which is a more grievous persecution.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, and it is in consequence, first and foremost, of ease and comfort, that those who are not vigilant undergo this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or thinkest thou it is a trifling persecution to be living at ease?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is more grievous than all, this is worse than persecution.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, like a running flux, ease makes the soul languid (χαυνοἵ): and as summer and winter, so persecution and ease.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But to show you that this is the worse persecution, listen: it induces sleep in the soul, an excessive yawning and drowsiness, it stirs up the passions on every side, it arms pride, it arms pleasure, it arms anger, envy, vainglory, jealousy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But in time of persecution none of these is able to make a disturbance; but fear, entering in, and plying the lash vigorously, as one does to a barking dog, will not let any of these passions so much as attempt to give tongue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who shall be able in time of persecution to indulge in vainglory?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who to live in pleasure?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not one: but there is much trembling and fear, making a great calm, composing the harbor into stillness, filling the soul with awe.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I have heard from our fathers (for in our own time God grant it may not happen, since we are bidden not to ask for temptation), that in the persecution of old time one might see men that were indeed Christian.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: None of them cared for money, none for wife, none for children, nor home, nor country: the one great concern with all was to save their lives (or, souls).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There were they hiding, some in tombs and sepulchres, some in deserts: yes tender and dainty women too, fighting all the while with constant hunger.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then think whether any longing for sumptuous and dainty living at all came into the mind of a woman, while in hiding beside a coffin (παρὰ λάρνακι), and waiting for her maid-servant to bring her meal, and trembling lest she should be taken, and lying in her terror as in a furnace: was she even aware that there ever was such a thing as dainty living, that such things as dress and ornaments exist at all (ὅτι κόσμος ὅλως ἐστίν)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou that now is the persecution, with our passions, like wild beasts, setting upon us on every side?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now is the trying persecution, both in this regard, and especially if it is not even thought to be persecution at all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this (persecution) has also this evil in it, that being war, it is thought to be peace, so that we do not even arm ourselves against it, so that we do not even rise: no one fears, no one trembles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if ye do not believe me, ask the heathen, the persecutors, at what time was the conduct of the Christians more strict, at what time were they all more proved?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Few indeed had they then become in number, but rich in virtue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, what profit is it, that there should be hay in plenty, when there might be precious stones?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The amount consists not in the sum of numbers, but in the proved worth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Elias was one: yet the whole world was not worth so much as he.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet the world consists of myriads: but they are no myriads, when they do not even come up to that one.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Better is one that doeth the will of God, than ten thousand who are transgressors:” for the ten thousands have not yet reached to the one.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Desire not a multitude of unprofitable children.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Ecclus. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such bring more blasphemy against God, than if they were not Christians.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What need have I of a multitude?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is (only) more food for the fire.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This one might see even in the body, that better is moderate food with health, than a (fatted) calf with damage.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is more food than the other: this is food, but that is disease.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This too one may see in war: that better are ten expert and brave men, than ten thousand of no experience.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These latter, besides that they do no work, hinder also those that do work.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The same too one may see to be the case in a ship, viz. that better are two experienced mariners, than ever so great a number of unskilful ones: for these will sink the ship.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things I say, not as looking with an evil eye upon your numbers, but wishing that all of you should be approved men, and not trust in your numbers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many more in number are they who go down into hell: but greater than it is the Kingdom, however few it contain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As the sand of the sea was the multitude of the people (Israel) yet one man saved them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moses was but one, and yet he availed more than they all: Joshua was one and he was enabled to do more than the six hundred thousand.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not make this our study merely, that (the people) may be many, but rather, that they may be excellent; when this shall have been effected, then will that other follow also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No one wishes at the outset to make a spacious house, but he first makes it strong and sure, then spacious: no one lays the foundations so that he may be laughed at.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us first aim at this, and then at the other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where this is, that also will be easy: but where this is not, the other, though it be, is to no profit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if there be those who are able to shine in the Church, there will soon be also numbers: but where these are not, the numbers will never be good for anything.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How many, suppose you, may there be in our city who are likely to be saved (τοὺς σωζομένους)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is disagreeable, what I am going to say, but I will say it nevertheless.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Among all these myriads, there are not to be found one hundred likely to be saved: nay, even as to these, I question it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For think, what wickedness there is in the young, what supineness in the aged!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: None makes it his duty to look after his own boy, none is moved by anything to be seen in his elder, to be emulous of imitating such an one.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The patterns are defaced, and therefore it is that neither do the young become admirable in conduct.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tell not me, “We are a goodly multitude:” this is the speech of men who talk without thought or feeling (ψυχρὥν.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the concerns of men indeed, this might be said with some show of reason: but where God is concerned, (to say this with regard to Him) as having need of us, can never be allowed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, let me tell you, even in the former case, this is a senseless speech (ψυχρόν).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A person that has a great number of domestics, if they be a corrupt set what a wretched time will he have of it!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For him who has none, the hardship, it seems, amounts to this, that he is not waited on: but where a person has bad servants, the evil is, that he is ruining himself withal, and the damage is greater (the more there are of them.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is far worse than having to be one’s own servant, to have to fight with others, and take up a (continual) warfare.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things I say, that none may admire the Church because of its numbers, but that we may study to make the multitude proof-worthy; that each may be earnest for his own share of the duty—not for his friends only, nor his kindred as I am always saying, nor for his neighbors, but that he may attract the strangers also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For example, Prayer is going on; there they lie (on bended knees), all the young, stupidly unconcerned (ψυχροὶ), (yes,) and old too: filthy nuisances rather than young men; giggling, laughing outright, talking—for I have heard even this going on—and jeering one another as they lie along on their knees: and there stand you, young man or elder: rebuke them, if you see them (behaving thus): if any will not refrain, chide him more severely: call the deacon, threaten, do what is in your power to do: and if he dare do anything to you, assuredly you shall have all to help you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For who is so irrational, as, when he sees you chiding for such conduct, and them chidden not to take your part?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Depart, having received your reward from the Prayer.—In a master’s house, we count those his best-disposed servants, who cannot bear to see any part of his furniture in disorder.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Answer me; if at home you should see the silver plate lie tossed out of doors, though it is not your business, you will pick it up and bring it into the house: if you see a garment flung out of its place, though you have not the care of it, though you be at enmity with him whose business it is, yet, out of good-will to the master, will you not put it right?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So in the present case.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These are part of the furniture: if you see them lying about in disorder, put them to rights: apply to me, I do not refuse the trouble: inform me, make the offender known to me: it is not possible for me to see all: excuse me (in this).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See, what wickedness overspreads the whole world!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Said I without reason that we are (no better than) so much hay (disorderly as) a troubled sea?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I am not talking of those (young people), that they behave thus; (what I complain of, is) that such a sleepy indifference possesses those who come in here, that they do not even correct this misbehavior.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again I see others stand talking while Prayer is going on; while the more consistent of them (do this) not only during the Prayer, but even when the Priest is giving the Benediction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When shall there be salvation? when shall it be possible for us to propitiate God?—Soldiers go to their diversion, and you shall see them, all keeping time in the dance, and nothing done negligently, but, just as in embroidery and painting, from the well-ordered arrangement in each individual part of the composition, there results at once an exceeding harmony and good keeping, so it is here: we have one shield, one head, all of us (in common): and if but some casual point be deranged by negligence, the whole is deranged and is spoilt, and the good order of the many is defeated by the disorder of the one part.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, fearful indeed to think of, here you come, not to a diversion, not to act in a dance, and yet you stand disorderly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Know you not that you are standing in company with angels? with them you chant, with them sing hymns, and do you stand laughing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is it not wonderful that a thunderbolt is not launched not only at those (who behave thus), but at us?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For such behavior might well be visited with the thunderbolt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Emperor is present, is reviewing the army: and do you, even with His eyes upon you, stand laughing, and endure to see another laughing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How long are we to go on chiding, how long complaining?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ought not such to be treated as very pests and nuisances; as abandoned, worthless reprobates, fraught with innumerable mischiefs, to be driven away from the Church?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When will these forebear laughing, who laugh in the hour of the dread Mystery (ἑν ὣρᾳ φρίκης)? when refrain from their trifling, who talk at the instant of the Benediction?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Have they no sense of shame before those who are present? have they no fear of God?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Are our own idle thoughts not enough for us, is it not enough that in our prayers we rove hither and thither, but laughter also must needs intrude, and bursts of merriment?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is it a theatrical amusement, what is done here?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Aye, but, methinks, it is the theatres that do this: to the theatres we owe it that the most of you so refuse to be curbed by us, and to be reformed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What we build up here, is thrown down there: and not only so, but the hearers themselves cannot help being filled with other filthinesses besides: so that the case is just the same as if one should want to clean out a place with a fountain above it discharging mire; for however much you may clean out, more runs in.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So it is here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when we clean people out, as they come here from the theatres with their filthiness, thither they go again, and take in a larger stock of filthiness, as if they lived for the purpose of only giving us trouble, and then come back to us, laden with ordure, in their manners, in their movements, in their words, in their laughter, in their idleness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then once more we begin shovelling it out afresh, as if we had to do this only on purpose that, having sent them away clean, we may again see them clogging themselves with filth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore I solemnly protest to you, the sound members, that this will be to you judgment and condemnation, and I give you over to God from this time forth, if any having seen a person behaving disorderly, if any having seen any person talking, especially in that part (of the Service), shall not inform against him, not bring him round (to a better behavior).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To do this is better than prayer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Leave thy prayer and rebuke him, that thou mayst both do him good, and thyself get profit, and so we may be enabled all to be saved and to attain unto the Kingdom of Heaven, through the grace and loving-kindness of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, dominion, honor, now and ever, and world without end.
